primary_diagnosis.name: Venous leg ulcer with acute secondary cellulitis

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic venous hypertension causes lower-leg oedema, haemosiderin staining and lipodermatosclerosis ('inverted champagne bottle'), and a shallow, irregular ulcer typically over the medial malleolus whose dull ache is relieved by elevation — the hallmark of a venous ulcer. Non-compliance with compression in hot weather has worsened venous congestion, and the ulcer has now developed acute secondary cellulitis: a new tender, warm, spreading peri-wound erythema with increased malodorous exudate and throbbing pain. Discriminators: palpable pedal pulses, medial-malleolar location, venous skin changes and pain relieved by elevation distinguish it from an arterial ulcer (painful, punched-out, pulseless, worse on elevation) — importantly, elevation is CORRECT here whereas it is harmful in arterial disease; the significant throbbing pain and venous staining argue against a (painless) neuropathic diabetic ulcer; and the clear chronic venous background without a rapidly expanding undermined violaceous border argues against pyoderma gangrenosum. Management is antibiotics for the cellulitis, wound cleansing/absorbent dressing, LEG ELEVATION now, analgesia, and community wound-care referral; compression therapy is resumed only after the acute infection/severe pain settle AND arterial disease is excluded by ABPI. Must-not-miss: spreading cellulitis/sepsis and deep infection/osteomyelitis (screened negative here) — so a strong sepsis safety-net is essential.

differential.diagnosis: Venous leg ulcer with secondary cellulitis
differential.evidence_basis: Medial-malleolar ulcer with venous skin changes, elevation-relief, now with peri-wound cellulitis.
Palpable pulses, medial location and elevation-relief argue against arterial (which worsens on elevation and is pulseless).
Neuropathic ulcers are painless plantar-pressure-point ulcers — this is painful and malleolar.
No rapidly expanding undermined violaceous border; clear venous cause.

differential.diagnosis: Spreading cellulitis / sepsis or deep infection
differential.evidence_basis: Screen for ascending cellulitis/fever/systemic toxicity and deep infection/osteomyelitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise a venous ulcer with secondary cellulitis; antibiotics + ELEVATION (not compression during acute infection); screen for sepsis/deep infection; exclude arterial disease (ABPI) before compression.

differential.diagnosis: Venous leg ulcer with secondary cellulitis
differential.evidence_basis: Localised peri-wound cellulitis on a venous ulcer; no ascending sepsis.

differential.diagnosis: Spreading cellulitis / sepsis or deep infection
differential.evidence_basis: Currently localised, but remains the key safety-net (and osteomyelitis needs consideration if not healing).

differential.diagnosis: Venous leg ulcer with secondary cellulitis
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis: antibiotics + elevation + wound care + community wound-clinic referral; compression later after infection settles and ABPI; sepsis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary cellulitis of the ulcer (new tender warm spreading peri-wound erythema, increased malodorous exudate, throbbing pain)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Needs antibiotics; can progress to systemic infection.

red_flags.description: Spreading cellulitis/sepsis (rapidly ascending calf redness, high fever/rigors, leg-wide swelling) or deep infection/osteomyelitis

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Resuming tight compression during acute infection/severe pain, or before excluding arterial disease (ABPI).
Missing the venous features (elevation-relief, pulses, staining) — note elevation is correct here, unlike arterial ulcers.
Not screening/safety-netting for ascending cellulitis, sepsis or osteomyelitis.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Antibiotics for the cellulitis, wound cleansing/absorbent dressing, LEG ELEVATION, analgesia, and urgent community wound-care referral; ED for spreading cellulitis/sepsis. Compression only after infection settles and ABPI excludes arterial disease.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Advising compression during acute infection or before ABPI, or missing ascending cellulitis/sepsis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Venous ulcers: graduated compression is the mainstay AFTER arterial disease is excluded (ABPI) and acute infection controlled; treat cellulitis with antibiotics; elevation, wound care and specialist wound-clinic management.
